coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria in urine sample using carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
coredata.dc:creator: Bahari, Delnia
Babamiri, Bahareh
Salimi, Abdollah
Salimizand, Himen
coredata.dc:description: Development of sensitive and selective analytical method for accurate diagnosis of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria in biological samples is a challenge. Herein, we developed an ingenious ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for sensitive and selective detection of (Ab) bacteria based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between ortho-phenylenediamines carbon dot (o-CD), nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCND) as donor's species and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor. NCND that assembled onto the edge of graphene oxide (GO) exhibited quenched photoluminescence emission, and with the absorption of the modified o-CD with aptamer (o-CD-ssDNA) onto the graphene oxide surface the fluorescence of o-CD was efficiently quenched. The aptamer (ssDNA) as a biorecognition element is bound with A. baumannii specifically which releases the o-CD-ssDNA from GO and the recovery of the fluorescence signal of o-CD, while the fluorescence intensity of NCND only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal in ratiometric fluorescence assay. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I550 nm/I440nm) varied from 2.0 to 10.0 with the concentration of bacteria changing from 2.0 × 103 to 4.5 × 107 cfu/mL and the low detection limit of 3.0 × 102 cfu/mL (S/N = 3). The feasibility of the developed aptasensor for selective detection of A. baumannii in urine sample with satisfactory results was also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria
Ratiometric fluorescence
Aptasensor
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Graphene oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Covalent attachment of aptamer to the o-CDs
Assembly of the nanosystem and detection of target bacteria

xocs:item-toc-section-title.ce:italic: Characterization and properties investigation of o-CD and o-CD-aptamer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the sensing mechanism of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab)
Analytical performance of the assay for detection of target bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and Gauss fitting of particle size distribution of NCND, (B) UV–vis absorption of NCNDs and PL spectra of NCNDs with various excitation wavelengths, (C) XPS scanning spectrum of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen; XPS high resolution survey scan of (D) C1s, (E) N1s and (F) O1s region of CDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of NCND, (B) TEM image and Gauss fitting of particle size distribution of o-CD, (C) PL spectra of o-CD with various excitation wavelengths, (D) FT-IR spectra of o-CD, (E) UV–vis absorption of (a) aptamer-oCD, (b) aptamer and (c) o-CD (F) The fluorescent spectra of the coupled NCNDs and o-CD scanned at different excitation wavelengths.

(A, B) TEM images of the NCND/GO and NCND/GO/apt-o-CD, (C) FT-IR spectra of NCND (a), NCND/GO (b) and NCND/GO/apt-o-CD (c), (D) FL spectra of NCND (a), FL spectra of o-CD (b), FL response in the presence of NCND/GO/apt-o-CD and the absence of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria (c) and FL response in the presence of NCND/GO/apt-o-CD and Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria(d).

(A) Effect of concentration of NCND/GO, (B) Effect of incubation time between NCND/GO and o-CD-aptamer and (C) Effect of incubation time between Ab bacteria and related aptamer (o-CD-aptamer), (D) Effects of various target bacteria on the PL response, (E) PL responses of the proposed sensor in different concentrations of Ab bacteria, (F) The corresponding relationship between the PL intensity and the logarithm of Ab bacteria concentration.
(A) Synthesis of the NCND, (B) Assembly of the nanosystem and detection of Acinetobacter baumannii.
Comparison the analytical performance of different assays for Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria detection.

row.entry: Fluorescence- microfluidic platform with dual aptamer
Fluorescence-microfluidic platform lab on a disc

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery experiments of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria in urine sample by the aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria in urine sample using carbon dots as optical nanoprobes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, 66177-13446, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology,Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, 66177-13446, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of sensitive and selective analytical method for accurate diagnosis of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria in biological samples is a challenge. Herein, we developed an ingenious ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for sensitive and selective detection of (Ab) bacteria based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between ortho-phenylenediamines carbon dot (o-CD), nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCND) as donor's species and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor. NCND that assembled onto the edge of graphene oxide (GO) exhibited quenched photoluminescence emission, and with the absorption of the modified o-CD with aptamer (o-CD-ssDNA) onto the graphene oxide surface the fluorescence of o-CD was efficiently quenched. The aptamer (ssDNA) as a biorecognition element is bound with A. baumannii specifically which releases the o-CD-ssDNA from GO and the recovery of the fluorescence signal of o-CD, while the fluorescence intensity of NCND only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal in ratiometric fluorescence assay. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I550 nm/I440nm) varied from 2.0 to 10.0 with the concentration of bacteria changing from 2.0 × 103 to 4.5 × 107 cfu/mL and the low detection limit of 3.0 × 102 cfu/mL (S/N = 3). The feasibility of the developed aptasensor for selective detection of A. baumannii in urine sample with satisfactory results was also demonstrated.

An ingenious ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor developed for ultrasensitive and selective detection of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between ortho-phenylenediamines carbon dot (o-CD), as donor's, graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor and NCND as reference signal.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab), is an opportunistic pathogen that can cause nosocomial infections such as pneumonia, bacteremia and meningitis of the skin, urinary tract, blood and other soft tissues. Furthermore, this pathogen has multiple antimicrobial resistances that has restricted the curative options available for infected patients [1–4]. Therefore, the development of sensitive and rapid analytical tools for detection of Ab is a challenge. The most commonly methods used to detect bacteria are the bacterial culture, biochemical assays, nucleic acid-based diagnosis and antibody-based assays [4]. Even though these methods are overall effective, the method are time-consuming and require well-trained personnel and sophisticated instruments. For example, the bacterial culture is employed by many hospitals because of the simple cultivation and enumeration of bacteria, but this process is intrinsically time-consuming and labor-intensive [5]. Also, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) as a simple example of antibody-based bacterial detection assay involves antigen-antibody interactions [6]. This method required sophisticated equipment, trained personnel, expensive and labor-intensive biological systems to produce antibodies. Furthermore, antibodies are functional under specific environment and the stability of antibodies is affected by the ionic strength and pH [6–8]. Also, in polymerase chain reaction (PCR) assay as a simple and rapid technique any form of contamination of the sample can cause to misleading results [9,10]. So, development of sensitive, simple, rapid, cost-effective and feasible analytical techniques to detection of Acinetobacter baumannii is extremely important.

Aptamers in comparison to the other molecular probes like antibodies have unique merits such as high purity, good stability, low toxicity, good selectivity and high binding affinity that this features make them arresting biorecognition elements in biotechnology and bioanalysis [11–15]. A diversity of aptamer-based detection methods such as electrochemical [13,16], surface Enhanced Raman scattering [17], electroluminescence [18–20], field effect transistor [21] and fluorescence [22,23] have been applied for detection of bacteria, DNA, RNA, proteins, cancer cells, molecules, etc.

Among different aptasensing measuring methods, fluorescent technique has been scrutinized in many fields because of its advantages including good sensitivity, high selectivity, rapid analysis, and little damage to sample [24,25]. Ratiometric fluorescence analysis that measures the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths can effectively decrease or even eliminate erroneous signals from environmental changes, such as background light, path length and scattering, which offers more accuracy and sensitivity for bioanalysis. So, ratiometric fluorescence as an significant and powerful analytical approach has attracted widespread consideration for the quantitative determination of the trace amounts of targets [26].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which involves the direct transfer of non-radiative excitation energy from an exited donor fluorophore to an acceptor, has attracted more attraction in developing of ratiometric fluorescence assay. Recently, different carbon-based materials including carbon dots (CDs), fluorescent graphene quantum dots, nanodiamonds and fullerene have been attracted as fluorophore owing to outstanding properties such as simple synthesis route, high photo stability, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [27–29]. Among all of these materials, carbon dots (CDs) as novel fluorescent probes by receiving the advantages as strong fluorescence, adjusted emission wavelength and abundant precursors are recognized as a promising candidate nanomaterial in FRET based assays [30].

Also, among the various energy acceptors used to fabricate FRET sensors, graphene with its large area conjugated structure, easy surface modification and higher quenching efficiency has attracted great attention to quench the fluorescence of many semi-conductor quantum dots (QDs) [31,32]. Furthermore, graphene oxide possesses very high fluorescence quenching efficiency and can interact with single-stranded ssDNA probe nonconvalently by hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions between the hexagonal cells of the graphene oxide and nucleotide bases. As a result, GO-based fluorescence assays has been explored broadly for the measurement of various types of targets [33–35].

In this study, sensitive and specific ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between o-CD, nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCND) as donor's species and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor has been developed for detection of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab). At first step amine riched-NCND covalently bonded to the carboxyl group on the edge of the GO using EDC and NHS as activating agents. Subsequently, o-CD containing carboxylic groups loaded with amine-terminated DNA (o-CD-ssDNA) through covalent bond by activation with NHS-EDC, adsorbed onto graphene oxide via the π-stacking interaction. As a result, the fluorescence of both NCND and o-CD was efficiently quenched by graphene oxide. In the presence of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria, o-CD-ssDNA which preferentially bound to the target bacteria release from the graphene oxide and the quenched fluorescence of o-CD turns “on” again, while the fluorescence intensity of CND was only slightly altered (Scheme 1 ). The ratio of the FL intensity (o-CD/NCND, I550nm/I440nm) increased with increasing Acinetobacter baumannii concentration at concentration range more than 4.5 orders. The proposed assay can be used for detection of Acinetobacter baumannii with good accuracy and high sensitivity with detection limit of 3.0 × 102 cfu/mL. The analytical performance of the proposed assay was investigated and its application for measuring of the analyte in urine as real sample was also evaluated.

Ortho-phenylenediamines, guanidinium chloride, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and NaCl were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Creatine, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamino-propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), ethanol, NaOH and HCl with analytical reagent grade were obtained from Merck or Fluka. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off = 3500 Da) were also obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab)-specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) aptamer was previously identified with SELEX method that their base sequence is summarized below [4]:
5′-NH2-ACAGCACCACAGACCACATATCACATGCTGTCGCCTTGCGATATCAATTCC.
AGTGATGTTTGTCTTCCTGCC-3′
The Ab-specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) aptamer synthesized by BIONEER, Global Genomics Partner. The aptamer was dissolved in double distilled water (ddH2O) and stored at −24 °C prior to use.

Optical absorption spectra were obtained with a UV–vis spectroscopy (Varian Cary 5000 UV-vis-NIR absorption spectrometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) by using a 10 mm quartz cuvette. LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA) was used to measure fluorescence spectra. Spectral band pass for both the light source and the detector with excitation wavelength at 350 nm were set to 10 nm and the detector applied voltage was 900 V. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was carried out using the KBr pellet technique on a Vector-22 BRUKER spectrophotometer (Switzerland). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were obtained from a H600 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). The shape, surface uniformity and size distribution of CDs were measured by HR-TEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 electron spectrometer using Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV) as a mono X-ray source.

The NCNDs were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of guanidinium chloride and creatine. Briefly, 0.655 mg creatine and 0.48 mg guanidinium chloride were added in 5 mL aqueous solution containing 0.05 g of NaOH under vigorous stirring until a clear solution was obtained. Then, the mixture was conveyed into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate large or agglomerated particles. The prepared C-dots was dialyzed against deionized (DI) water with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag for two days to eliminate the unreacted precursor and small molecular products. Then, the obtained homogeneous light yellow suspension was stored at 4 °C for further use.

o-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal treatment according to the reported method with minor modification. Typically, 0.3 g ortho-phenylenediamines was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol followed by being stirred uniformly. Then, the prepared solution is sealed in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The orange product was purified via centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the subsidence. Then, the o-CD solution was dried under vacuum and to further purify the o-CD, the obtained product dissolved in 5 mL water was dialyzed against deionized (DI) water for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent attachment of aptamer to the o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The 5׳-amine modified of the aptamer (ssDNA) was covalently attached to the carboxyl group of the o-CD using EDC/NHS as activating agents. Briefly, 1 mL of the o-CD (5 mg/mL) solution was activated with a solution containing EDC (400 μL, 2 mg/mL) and NHS (200 μL, 2 mg/mL) at room temperature for 2 h, followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min. Finally, 50 μL of 500 nM Ab aptamer solution (pH 7.4) was mixed with the above solution. The concentration of aptamer in prepared solution is 15 nM. With continuous stirring at 37 °C for 12 h and the prepared o-CDs-aptamer were centrifuged, washed twice to remove unbound sequence, and re-dispersed in 1 mL PBS buffer.

The Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacterium ATCC 19606 was cultured in Luria-Bertani broth (Himedia, India) at 37 °C for 18 h with shaking at 200 rpm. Bacterial cells were harvested through centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min and then rinsed twice with 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4). The optical density of microbial cultures was obtained at λ = 600 nm (OD600) to adjust the concentration of bacteria to 0.4 cfu/mL using spectrophotometric method. Plate-counting method was used for measurement of the bacteria cell numbers. It is necessary that all the containers were sterilized before and after of using.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the nanosystem and detection of target bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene oxide was covalently bound to the NH2 group of the NCND according to the following procedure: 1 mL of the GO (0.4 mg/mL) solution was activated with 1 mL of EDC (20 mg/mL) and 1 mL NHS (10 mg/mL) for 30 min. Then, the activated GO were mixed with 1 mL of NCND and was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. The prepared NCND/GO was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 30 min) to remove unbound NCND. In the follow, 0.5 mg/mL of the obtained NCND/GO were added into the 200 μL of aptamer modified/o-CD for 30 min, followed by centrifugation and redispersing in 1 mL PBS. Finally, various concentrations of the bacteria were added to the NCND/GO/apt-o-CD assembly and after 30 min incubation in PBS buffer, fluorescence spectrum of each sample was recorded and variation of fluorescence ratio values of o-CD/NCND signal (I550 nm/I440nm) versus Ab concentration applied as measured signal.

To investigate of shape, size, and surface uniformity of the luminescent NCNDs, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied for characterization. Fig. 1 A illustrates the TEM image of the as-prepared NCNDs, which displays that NCNDs are monodispersed with an average size of 5–8 nm. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded to characterize the optical properties of NCNDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the NCNDs aqueous solution has a considerable absorption band centered at approximately 320 nm, which most probably corresponded to the n-π* transition of CO bond. The fluorescence emission spectra of the NCNDs recorded at various excitation wavelengths from 200 nm to 450 nm and the maximum fluorescence emission intensity was observed at 440 nm. Also, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterization was performed to further confirm of the presented functional groups in the synthesized sample. In the XPS spectrum of the NCNDs, three characteristic peaks observed at 282.36, 398.18 and 528.38 eV are considered as the C1s, N1s and O1s binding peak, respectively (Fig. 1C). In the expanded XPS spectrum, the C1s peaks (Fig. 1D) at 284.95, 286.35, 287.85 and 288.85 eV can be assigned to carbon in the form of C–C/C–H (sp3), C–OH/C–O–C (sp3), CO (sp2) and O–CO (sp2), respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) can be de-convoluted into three surface components corresponding to –NH2 functional group at 399.15 eV, N-(CO)–O at 400.55eVand N–C3 at 401.25eV. The obtained results indicated that the synthesized carbon dots are nitrogen-doped CDs (NCNDs). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1F, the high resolution spectrum of O1s indicated the main peaks at 531.15, 532.95 and 533.94 eV corresponding to N–CO, C–O–C and O–CO*, respectively. The infrared spectrum of the NCNDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The observed peaks at 1164 cm−1 and 1342 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–O–C and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the absorption bands of C–N and CO located at 1506 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1, respectively, which indicated the existence of amide groups in prepared NCND. The absorption bands at 3020 and 2843 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of CH2, respectively. Moreover, the broad peak located at ~3422 cm−1 corresponds to stretching of the amino group (-NH2).

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Characterization and properties investigation of o-CD and o-CD-aptamer

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized o-CD was characterized by TEM technique. As shown in Fig. 2B, the average size of the uniform nanoparticles is in the range 6–10 nm with the mean particle size of nearly 8 nm. The UV–vis spectrum of o-CDs showed a significant absorption band centered at approximately 420 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of the o-CD recorded at various excitation wavelengths from 250 to 480 nm recorded and the maximum fluorescence emission intensity was at 550 nm (Fig. 2C). The IR-spectrum recorded for to characterization of the chemical structure of synthesized o-CD as shown in Fig. 2D. The infrared peaks at 2925-2858 cm−1 and 1386-1328 cm−1 can be attributed to aliphatic C–H and C–N = stretching vibrations, respectively, and the peak at 1269 and 1631 cm−1 correspond to C–O and CO stretching vibration. The successful immobilization of aptamer (ssDNA) on o-CD was also investigated by studying the optical absorption spectra of o-CD, aptamer (ssDNA) and o-CDs-aptamer. As illustrated in Fig. 2E, o-CD and aptamer (ssDNA) demonstrate maximum absorption peak at about 420 nm and 240 nm respectively. With immobilization aptamer (ssDNA) on o-CD, the same peaks at 420 nm and 240 nm were observed that denoting the successful immobilization of aptamer (ssDNA) onto o-CD. Furthermore, Fig. 2F illustrate the obtained results for simultaneous excitation wavelength scanning of NCND and o-CD. As shown, with excitation wavelength at 350 nm, NCND and o-CD show the strong emission in 440 nm and 550 nm, respectively without any disorder in the maxima or intensity. Therefore, 350 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength both of CDs for the subsequent experiments.

In this study, graphene oxide (GO) with –COOH functional groups was covalently conjugated to NH2 groups of NCND, then the apt-o-CD were assembled upon the NCND/GO via the π-stacking interaction. Therefore, to examine the successful immobilization of NCND and apt-o-CD, TEM images of the NCND/GO and NCND/GO/apt-o-CD were employed (Figure 3 A and B). As can be seen, NCNDs were preferentially immobilized onto the GO edge by covalent interaction because of high density of carboxyl groups located there (Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows TEM image of the NCND/GO/apt-o-CD that proved the π-stacking interaction between the aromatic bases of the aptamer and the hexagonal cells of GO. These results confirm the attachment of NCND-NH2 on the GO edges and also the immobilization of apt-o-CD onto the GO surface. Furthermore, the FTIR spectroscopy was employed to investigate immobilization of NCND and apt-o-CD onto the GO. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3C), characteristic bands of 3422, 1653 and 1500 cm−1 corresponding to stretch of the amino group (NH2), stretch vibration of amide I on NCND, respectively, appeared upon the GO/NCND. Also, the peak at 1087 cm−1 (assigned to p-o stretching vibration) on the aptamer indicated the assembly of apt-o-CD onto NCND/GO. The obtained results confirmed that the NCND/GO and NCND/GO/apt-o-CD was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the sensing mechanism of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab)
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3D, the NCND and o-CD had the strong emission in 440 nm and 550 nm, respectively (curve a, b). After the successive immobilization of NCND onto the GO edge, due to the efficacious quenching effect of GO, the fluorescence signal of NCND at 440 nm was efficiently quenched. Herein, decoration the GO edge with NCND, retain the intact basal plane of GO to adsorb a high density of apt-oCD. With the absorption of o-CD modified with aptamer (o-CD-ssDNA) onto the graphene oxide surface via the π-stacking interaction, the fluorescence of o-CD was also efficiently quenched (curve c). In the presence of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) bacteria, specifically interaction between A. baumannii and apt-o-CD led to the release of the apt-o-CD and the recovery of the fluorescence signal of o-CD, while the fluorescence intensity of NCND was only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal in ratiometric fluorescence assay (curve d). So, the combination of PL with RET can be employed as novel switchable technique for determination of bacteria. Fig. 3D reveals a blue shift for both NCND and o-CD fluorescence signal, when compared with the individual NCND and o-CD. This result likely reflects significant interactions of NCND and o-CD with the graphene oxide. Actually, the dehydration reaction causes the amino to be substituted by amide, and the defect state of the NCND surface changes, the blue emission is assigned to amide-containing fluorophore. Also, with the absorption of o-CD modified with aptamer (o-CD-ssDNA) onto the graphene oxide surface via the π- π stacking interaction, chemical environments and corresponding fluorescence emission position of o-CD changed. Furthermore, the blue shift of the o-CD in the aptasensor is probably due to hydrogen bonding between polar units at the o-CD surface and hydroxyl groups within the graphene oxide [36]. The interaction of fluorophores with graphene oxide may change the distribution of emissive trap sites on the o-CD and NCND surfaces, which reflected in a slight blue shift of the PL emission spectra of both o-CD and NCND when they are together compared to individuals. The obtained results are in good agreement with previous reported results for observing of blue shift during interaction of CDs with other materials [36–38].

To achieve the excellent performance of the FL ratiometric aptasensor for the detection of Ab bacteria, several experimental conditions including the concentration of NCND/GO, incubation time between NCND/GO and o-CD-aptamer and also the incubation time between Ab bacteria and related aptamer (o-CD-aptamer) were investigated. To optimize the concentration of complementary NCND/GO and o-CD-aptamers, varying concentrations (0–1.0 mg/mL) of NCND/GO were added into 200 μL of o-CD-aptamer. With increasing NCND/GO, the fluorescence intensity of o-CD-aptamers decreased and then tended to a constant value. Thus, 0.5 mg/mL of NCND/GO was selected as the definitive concentration of NCND/GO in this study (Fig. 4 A). Furthermore, for optimization of o-CD-aptamer concentration, 0.5 mg/mL of NCND/GO was added to different volumes of o-CD-aptamer (50–300 μL) and their fluorescence spectra were recorded (not shown). The results indicate at O-CD-aptamer concentration more than 200 μL the fluorescence response is level off. Also, incubation time between NCND/GO and o-CD-aptamer was studied by recording of fluorescence signal between 5 and 50 min. As shown in Fig. 4B, with increasing incubation time, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased and reached the minimum value at 25 min. So, 25 min was selected as the optimal incubation time in this experiment. Furthermore, the effect of incubation time of bacteria with aptamer on the restoration of fluorescence was also studied. The obtained result indicated that with increasing of incubation time, the fluorescence intensity increased and then remained stable after 20 min in the presence of bacteria (Fig. 4C). The obtained result illustrated that the o-CD-aptamer@Ab bacteria complex is formed completely within 20 min, and it was chosen as the definite incubation time of Ab bacteria in this experiment.

To examine whether fluorescence probe can selectively detect Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria, we treated NCND/GO/o-CD-aptamer with various non-target bacteria such as Escherichia coli O157:H7, Clabsi bacteria and Sau bacteria in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and incubated the solution for 30 min. The fluorescence response of the probe toward Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria (10.0 × 106 cfu/mL) and non-target bacteria with concentration of 20.0 × 107 cfu/mL were recorded (Fig. 4D). As shown the proposed assay showed excellent selectivity toward Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria compared to other bacteria when their concentrations were 20.0 folds of the target bacteria. These results indicated that the FL ratiometric aptasensor had excellent specificity for Ab bacteria detection. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of the five independent assays and its application for detection of fixed concentration of the bacteria (40 cfu/mL) was 4.51%, that indicating an acceptable precision and reproducibility of the proposed assay. To investigate the long-time stability of the proposed aptasensor for the determination of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab), fluorescence intensity was recorded over a period of 4 weeks, the sensor was stored in 4 °C when not in use. It was found that after a storage period of 4 weeks, over 90% of the initial response remained. The obtained results indicated that the proposed aptasensor has desirable reliability and stability and it can be used for a sufficiently long time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the assay for detection of target bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of NCND/GO-based aptasensor in the presence of various concentrations of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab) and the related calibration plot for the Ab bacteria measurement are illustrated in Fig. 4E. As illustrated, the fluorescence intensity of the o-CDs at 550 nm gradually increased with increasing bacteria concentration from 2.0 × 103 to 4.5 × 107 cfu/mL. By increasing the distance between the graphene surface as the acceptor and aptamer-o-CD as the donor, the fluorescence intensity of o-CD regenerated, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the NCNDs at 440 nm wasn't significantly changed. The result confirms that due to high affinity between o-CD-aptamers and target bacteria, aptamer-o-CD release from GO surface and restoration of fluorescence signal was obtained. The linear regression equations for bacteria detection was shown in Fig. 4F. The calculated detection limit was 3.0 × 102 cfu/mL for Ab bacteria at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The low detection limit and the broad linear range of the developed fluorescence aptasensor indicated that the proposed strategy can be used as a highly sensitive approach in comparison the other quantitative method [4,39] for the determination of Ab bacteria. In addition, the process of detection of bacteria can be completed within 20 min, so presented work was faster than conventional techniques such as colony culture and counting, PCR, ELISA, [40,41]. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 the calibration concentration range and detection limit of the proposed sensing method is better or comparable with other reported assays. Due to low detection limit and linear calibration range of the proposed aptasensor it can be developed as suitable assay for environmental testing and clinical recognitions [42].

To investigate the possibility of the constructed fluorescent aptasensor toward selective detection of A. baumannii target in complex samples, urine was used as the model sample. As can be seen in Table 2 , the recoveries were in the range of 96.84–101.87% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were less than 5.0%. The obtained results indicated that the fabricated fluorescent aptasensor can be used for the selective detection of A. baumannii in real samples, and it can be developed as novel ratiometric fluorescence assay for analysis of other bacteria or biomarkers.

In summary, the ratiometric aptasensor via FRET between o-CD and NCND) as donor's species and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor has been developed for detection of Acinetobacter baumannii (Ab). The GO was assembled with NCND and the modified o-CD with aptamer to construct a fluorescence ratiometric biosensor. The desorption/adsorption of aptamer onto the surface of GO has enable the nanoprobe to detect the bacteria in the concentration range of 2.0 × 103 cfu/mL to 4.5 × 107 cfu/mL and exhibits high sensitivity with a detection limit as low as 3.0 × 102 cfu/mL. This method with low detection limit has opened new attitudes for improving traditional and available detection methods with more sensitivity, high selectivity and short time analysis. Therefore, the presented ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor oﬀers a new approach to construct convenient, high sensitivity and speciﬁcity, more stability, holding great potential for pathogenic bacteria detection in real samples. Undoubtedly, the ratiometric aptasensor offers great potential for Acinetobacter baumannii detection in urine sample as practical application. Given the sensitivity, selectivity and ease of use of this aptamer, the proposed biosensor may hold great promise for clinical diagnostics of A. baumannii and it is promised as novel ratiometric fluorescence method for sensing of other bacteria or biomarkers and diagnostics of related diseases.

Delnia Bahari, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Bahareh Babamiri, Methodology, Investigation. Abdollah Salimi, Supervision, writing and review. Himen Salimizand, Investigation, Preparation of Bacteria Strains.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative and the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number.401261).
